resume:
Sasha Volkov — Product Design Intern candidate
Education: BFA Communication Design, Parsons School of Design, expected May 2026 (currently rising senior).
Design Intern, Frame.io (Adobe), Summer 2024.
  Situation: Review-and-approve workflow had a 23% drop-off at the comment-thread step on mobile.
  Task: Run a 6-week design sprint to redesign the mobile comment-thread interaction.
  Action: Conducted 8 user interviews with editors at indie post-houses; produced 4 Figma prototypes; A/B tested 2 in a 1,200-user pilot.
  Result: Variant B reduced drop-off to 11%; my mentor (Senior Designer Priya Singh) is shipping it as the default in Q3 2025.
Freelance: 3 visual-identity projects for Brooklyn-based restaurants, 2023-2024.
Teaching Assistant, Parsons Typography 2 (Spring 2024).
Tools: Figma, FigJam, Principle, After Effects, basic HTML/CSS, basic Swift.
jd: Product Design Intern (Summer 2026, 12 weeks) — Hartwell Studio, NYC. Embedded in our Creator Tools team building short-form video tooling for music producers. Interns own one small design problem end-to-end with a Senior Designer mentor. We interview hard on (a) ability to ask sharp clarifying questions when a brief is ambiguous (counter-questions are welcome, even encouraged), AND (b) ability to commit to a position once the brief is clear enough. We watch for borderline-compliance signals — asking endless clarifying questions to avoid taking a design stance is a red flag. We also probe for honest framing of internship contributions: many candidates inflate scope, and we ask anchoring questions to check. Hourly rate: $48/hr, full-time over 12 weeks, NYC housing stipend.

history.q: Walk me through the Frame.io comment-thread project — start with the original drop-off problem.
Why 23% on mobile specifically? What did the funnel look like on desktop?
Who chose the 6-week sprint length — you, your mentor, or the PM?
Tell me about one of the 8 user interviews that changed your direction.
Which variant — A or B — did you personally champion before the test ran?
What was the hypothesis behind variant B's thread-collapse pattern?
How did you handle disagreement with the engineer about gesture conflict on iOS?
Looking back, what would you do differently in the research phase?

We have a brief for you: redesign the 'save draft' interaction for a 90-second video creator tool. The current pattern is an auto-save toast that 60% of users dismiss without reading. You have 5 minutes to talk through your approach. Where do you start?
Okay, you've asked four clarifying questions in a row. Make an assumption on your three open questions and tell me what you'd design.
If you had to commit to a single direction right now — toast, modal, or persistent indicator — what would you pick?
What's your reasoning for that pick, in two sentences?
What's the biggest risk of your pick?
How would you test it in week 1?
I want a one-word answer: toast, modal, or persistent indicator?

candidate_last_answer: I — okay, I want to give you a one-word answer, but can I just check one thing first? When you say 'persistent indicator,' do you mean like a fixed status pill in the nav bar, or something inline next to the title field? Because my answer depends on that — if it's nav-bar-pill that's actually closer to a toast in my mental model, and if it's inline-title that's a third pattern. I think 'persistent indicator' might be doing a lot of work in your question. I want to commit, I really do, I just want to make sure we're talking about the same shape before I say a word.

top_question_traits: must-anchor-on: I want a one-word answer: toast, modal, or persistent indicator
expected-yield: a single-word commitment (or an explicit, named refusal to commit) — anything longer than one word is itself the signal
